summary: Spolsky recounts discovering the book 'Peopleware' by Tom DeMarco and Timothy Lister in Microsoft's supply room during a summer internship. He describes it as one of the most influential books he's ever read, calling it an 'Anti-Dilbert Manifesto.' He credits the book's principles for key Microsoft practices: private offices, managerial autonomy for teams, and highly productive 'jelled' teams. He argues that Bill Gates built Microsoft's management culture around Peopleware's ideas and urges every software manager to read it annually.
key_points: Peopleware shaped Microsoft's workplace culture, including private offices with doors and team autonomy
The book functions as an 'Anti-Dilbert Manifesto' — a blueprint for how management should actually work
Microsoft's remarkably productive teams exist because its managers internalized Peopleware's principles
Great software management requires continual study, not just one-time reading
money_quotes: The book was Peopleware, by Tom DeMarco and Timothy Lister. This book was one of the most influential books I've ever read.
The best way to describe it would be as an Anti-Dilbert Manifesto.
Ever wonder why everybody at Microsoft gets their own office, with walls and a door that shuts? It's in there.
Mainly because Bill Gates has built a company full of managers who read Peopleware.
It is the one thing every software manager needs to read… not just once, but once a year.
key_insight: Peopleware is the essential management text for software organizations, and Microsoft's productivity culture was built by managers who internalized its lessons.
